data.title: Goldman Sachs raises Q4 oil price outlook on reduced Middle East output
data.description:
• Goldman Sachs has increased its fourth-quarter oil price forecasts to $90 per barrel for Brent crude (CO1:COM) and $83 for West Texas Intermediate (CL1:COM), citing reduced output from the Middle East.
  • "The economic risks are larger than our ​crude base case alone suggests because of the ​net upside risks to oil prices, unusually ⁠high refined product prices, products shortages risks, and ​the unprecedented scale of the shock," GS analysts led ​by Daan Struyven said in an April 26 note, as per Reuters.
  • The forecast assumes a normalization in Gulf exports through the Strait of ​Hormuz by end-June versus mid-May previously and a ​slower Gulf production recovery.
  • GS estimates 14.5 million barrels per day of ‌Middle ⁠East crude production losses are driving global oil inventories to draw at a record 11-12 million bpd pace in April, as per the report.
  • GS expects the global oil market ​to swing ​from a ⁠1.8 million bpd 2025 surplus to a 9.6 million bpd Q2 2026 deficit. Global ​oil demand expected to fall 1.7 ​million bpd ⁠in Q2, 100,000 bpd in 2026 on year given jump in refined product prices.
  • "Because extreme inventory draws ⁠are ​not sustainable, even sharper demand ​losses could be required if the supply shock persists longer," the ​analysts said.

data.title: Noble Non-GAAP EPS of $0.26 beats by $0.05, revenue of $785.69M beats by $43.5M
data.description:
• Noble (NE): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.26 beats by $0.05.
  • Revenue of $785.69M (-10.2% Y/Y) beats by $43.5M.
  • Press Release

data.title: HSBC reviews $38,000 per-child school fee benefit for Hong Kong bankers: report
data.description:
• HSBC Holdings (HSBC) is reviewing a long-standing perk that covers school fees for a swath of bankers in Hong Kong as part of a broader push to standardize benefits globally and reduce costs, Bloomberg News reported.
  • The London-headquartered bank is considering options including scrapping the perk for new joiners or adjusting total compensation, the report said.
  • HSBC has been reviewing the benefit for some time, and no decisions have yet been made.
  • Under chief executive Georges Elhedery, HSBC is undertaking its most significant overhaul in a decade, marked by thousands of job cuts across divisions and a streamlining of management layers.

data.title: Keyence surges 16% to hit daily limit after strong earnings and buyback announcement
data.description:
• Keyence (KYCCF) jumped 16% on Monday, the biggest gain since August 2024, after the Japanese maker of factory automation sensors delivered quarterly operating income that exceeded analyst estimates.
  • The comany also said it will seek investor approval to amend its charter to enable buybacks.
  • The stock reached the daily limit after operating profit for the fourth quarter came in at ¥179.4B ($1.1B), surpassing Bloomberg consensus of ¥163.8B, due to higher overseas sales, according to a company filing Friday.

data.title: Sun Pharma to buy Organon in $11.75B deal, expanding global reach
data.description:
Sun Pharmaceutical Industries on Sunday said it agreed to acquire Organon & Co. (OGN) in an all-cash transaction valuing the company at an enterprise value of $11.75 billion, marking one of the largest healthcare deals of the year.

Under the agreement, Organon shareholders will receive $14 a share in cash. The transaction has been approved by the boards of both companies and is expected to close in early 2027, pending regulatory clearances and approval from Organon shareholders.

Organon (OGN) was formed in 2021 through a spinoff from Merck & Co. (MRK), known as MSD outside the United States and Canada. The company focuses on women’s health, established medicines and biosimilars, with products sold in about 140 countries.

Sun Pharma said the acquisition would broaden its international footprint and add new capabilities in women’s health and biosimilars. The combined company is expected to generate about $12.4 billion in revenue, placing it among the world’s larger pharmaceutical companies by sales.

The deal would also increase Sun Pharma’s exposure to branded generics and established medicines, while giving it a stronger presence in developed markets including the United States and Europe.

Sun Pharma plans to finance the acquisition through a mix of existing cash and committed bank financing. The company said post-transaction net debt to earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization is expected to be about 2.3 times.

For the year ended December 31, Organon reported revenue of $6.2 billion and adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization of $1.9 billion. The company had debt of $8.6 billion and cash of $574 million at year-end.

The acquisition comes as pharmaceutical companies pursue scale to offset pricing pressure, patent expirations and rising research costs. For investors, the transaction will likely be judged on Sun Pharma’s ability to integrate Organon’s portfolio, manage leverage and generate growth from its expanded global platform.

data.title: Oil climbs as U.S.-Iran talks stall, Hormuz flows stay constrained
data.description:
Oil prices moved higher on Monday after diplomatic efforts between the United States and Iran appeared to lose momentum, while limited shipments through the Strait of Hormuz continued to tighten global crude supplies.

Brent crude futures (CO1:COM) rose $2.20, or 2.1%, to $107.53 a barrel late in the session. U.S. West Texas Intermediate (CL1:COM) gained $2, or 2.1%, to $96.40 a barrel.

Traders have been closely monitoring the Strait of Hormuz, a critical chokepoint for global energy markets, where reduced flows have added to concerns about supply availability. Any sustained disruption in the waterway can quickly ripple through oil prices because a significant share of the world’s seaborne crude passes through the route.

The latest gains also reflected fading hopes for a near-term diplomatic breakthrough between Washington and Tehran. Markets had been watching for progress that could ease regional tensions and potentially improve the outlook for energy shipments.

With supply risks elevated and inventories already under pressure in some markets, oil prices remain highly sensitive to geopolitical headlines and shipping data.

data.title: Wall Street’s quantum dream meets reality as banks diverge
data.description:
Goldman Sachs (GS) and JPMorgan Chase (JPM) have adopted sharply different strategies in Wall Street’s race to capitalize on quantum computing, underscoring the uncertainty surrounding a technology often promoted as the next major breakthrough after artificial intelligence, Bloomberg News reported Sunday.

Goldman scaled back its efforts after internal research suggested current hardware was nowhere near capable of delivering practical investment applications. JPMorgan, by contrast, has continued investing heavily, maintaining a team of more than 50 physicists, mathematicians and computer scientists focused on potential uses across the bank.

A few years ago, Goldman appeared well positioned in the field, hiring specialists and partnering with Amazon.com to test whether quantum systems could improve portfolio returns for wealthy clients.

The results were sobering, according to the report.

Researchers found one potential algorithm would require millions of years to run and roughly 8 million logical qubits, far beyond today’s machines, many of which still operate with fewer than 100 qubits. Goldman later reduced its quantum program as part of broader cost-cutting measures.

JPMorgan has taken the opposite view, continuing research into applications such as portfolio construction, machine learning, pricing models and cybersecurity.

Quantum computing uses the principles of quantum mechanics to solve certain problems far faster than traditional computers. Supporters see potential in areas ranging from drug discovery and logistics to fraud detection and financial risk modeling, though many experts say commercial usefulness may still be years away.

Banks face a particular challenge because they are pursuing a wide range of possible uses, from asset pricing and portfolio optimization to encryption and fraud detection, making near-term returns harder to prove.

JPMorgan says it has already made progress. Last year it said it used Quantinuum hardware to process fast-moving data more efficiently for fraud monitoring and network analysis. It also demonstrated a portfolio-selection tool with Amazon aimed at improving diversification.

Otter said useful quantum applications could emerge within the next few years if hardware improves.

McKinsey estimated last year that quantum computing revenue could rise to as much as $72 billion by 2035 from roughly $4 billion in 2024.

Other banks are experimenting as well. UBS is training quant analysts, BBVA has explored portfolio optimization, Credit Agricole has studied credit-risk models and HSBC has tested anti-money laundering and bond-trading uses.

Still, many now acknowledge early expectations ran ahead of reality. Rigetti Computing Chief Executive Subodh Kulkarni said the industry promised more than it could deliver in the near term.

For now, Wall Street’s quantum push is less about profits than preparation. Goldman stepped back. JPMorgan is still betting that patience could eventually pay off.

data.title: SocGen sees credit markets holding firm despite Middle East shock
data.description:
Credit markets have shown surprising resilience during the latest geopolitical flare-up, even as rising oil prices, higher bond yields and renewed investor caution rattled equities, according to a new report from Société Générale strategist Juan Esteban Valencia.

Valencia on April 24 said credit spreads moved only modestly wider this week despite the continued closure of the Strait of Hormuz and slow-moving ceasefire negotiations in the Middle East. New bond issuance also remained active, suggesting investors still have appetite for corporate debt.

“Credit is well positioned to weather the storm, provided it does not rage for too long,” Valencia wrote in the bank’s weekly credit strategy note.

Strait closure keeps markets on edge

The report said markets remain focused on whether shipping traffic can resume through the Strait of Hormuz, a vital route for global energy flows. If the disruption drags on, SocGen expects additional pressure on oil prices and fresh supply-chain bottlenecks.

Brent crude climbed above $107 a barrel during the week, while West Texas Intermediate rose to nearly $98, according to the report. At the same time, European and U.S. stock indexes declined as investors rotated into defensive positions.

Credit spreads stay contained

Even with macro uncertainty building, corporate bond spreads remained relatively steady. Euro investment-grade spreads were around 92 basis points over benchmarks, while U.S. investment-grade spreads stood near the same level. High-yield markets also moved within narrow ranges.

SocGen said this suggests investors still view corporate balance sheets as healthy and default risks as manageable, at least for now.

Earnings offer some support

Another stabilizing factor has been earnings season. Valencia noted that many companies on both sides of the Atlantic have so far beaten expectations, with earnings growth running near 20% early in the reporting period.

However, he cautioned that first-quarter results may not yet fully capture the impact of higher energy costs or supply disruptions tied to the Middle East conflict.

What it means for investors

For investors, the report implies that corporate bonds may currently offer a relative safe haven compared with equities, particularly if geopolitical tensions remain contained. Credit investors can still collect yield while avoiding some of the volatility hitting stock markets.

SocGen said it remains overweight euro investment-grade debt relative to U.S. credit and high yield, while favoring sectors such as financials, utilities, real estate and corporate hybrids. Retail and senior non-financial debt remain less favored.

Busy week ahead

Looking forward, Valencia said markets will also be watching Federal Reserve and European Central Bank meetings, inflation data and a heavy slate of earnings reports. But he added that developments in the Middle East are still likely to be the dominant driver of sentiment.

data.title: Global central banks face war-driven inflation test as bond markets brace for signals
data.description:
Investors are heading into a pivotal week for global monetary policy, with the world’s leading central banks set to deliver interest-rate decisions against the backdrop of rising geopolitical tensions and renewed inflation concerns, Bloomberg News reported Sunday.

The Federal Reserve, European Central Bank, Bank of Japan, Bank of England and Bank of Canada are all scheduled to meet, creating a rare moment when every Group of Seven central bank is simultaneously weighing policy for economies that collectively represent roughly half of global output. Markets broadly expect officials to leave rates unchanged, but attention will focus on how policymakers describe inflation risks tied to the U.S.-Iran conflict and higher energy prices.

Any indication that central bankers are prepared to maintain restrictive policy for longer, or even tighten further, could pressure government bonds. Sovereign debt has lagged equities and credit markets in recent weeks as investors largely looked through the conflict and continued buying risk assets.

Amy Xie Patrick of Pendal Group said policymakers have little downside in sounding firm on inflation now, particularly given the uncertain outlook for oil prices and broader price pressures. She said she has removed duration exposure from her portfolio this month.

Bond markets have remained unusually calm despite the geopolitical backdrop. Yields on short-dated notes in major markets have stayed relatively elevated, while day-to-day moves in one- to three-year maturities have been smaller than in March. Some investors believe that calm could end if policymakers adopt a more forceful tone this week.

Stephen Miller, formerly of BlackRock in Australia and now a consultant at GSFM, said central banks remain wary of underestimating inflation after the policy mistakes made during the pandemic, when many officials initially described price pressures as temporary.

In the United Kingdom, Bank of England officials have already warned that the conflict could worsen inflation. U.K. consumer prices rose 3.3% in March from a year earlier, up from 3.0% in February, helped by higher fuel costs. Markets responded last week by shifting from pricing one rate increase this year to expecting at least two.

In the United States, Federal Reserve officials have acknowledged that sustained higher oil prices could complicate the inflation outlook and potentially affect the rate path. At the same time, resilient employment and retail sales data suggest the economy has not yet materially weakened.

TD Securities strategist Molly Brooks expects Fed Chair Jerome Powell to emphasize policy neutrality while acknowledging that recent energy-driven inflation pressures have added uncertainty. She said the firm expects the 10-year Treasury yield to remain in a roughly 4.1% to 4.4% range absent clearer guidance.

Elsewhere, Bank of Japan Governor Kazuo Ueda has stressed the need to weigh both upside and downside inflation risks, while analysts at Evercore ISI expect the BOJ to hold rates steady but maintain a hawkish bias. ECB President Christine Lagarde is also expected to reiterate that uncertainty remains high, even as markets continue to anticipate additional tightening in coming months.

Still, the inflation story is only part of the equation. If higher energy prices and geopolitical stress begin to undermine demand, central banks may eventually need to pivot toward growth concerns. That could ultimately pull yields lower after the current focus on inflation fades.
